coredata.dc:title: Solid/liquid hybrid lubrication behaviors of amorphous carbon film coupling with nonpolar and polar base oils
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Yanfei
Liu, Xiaoqiang
Yang, Jun
Zhang, Xinghua
coredata.dc:description: The amorphous carbon film (a-C) has aroused intensive interest in solid-oil hybrid lubrication design due to its low friction, high wear resistance, and good chemical inertness. However, the solid/liquid hybrid lubrication behaviors and mechanisms of a-C films when coupled with nonpolar and polar oils of similar viscosity are still insufficiently understood and ambiguous to date. In this study, we compared the lubrication properties of nonpolar paraffin oil and polar polyether oils with different molecular structures when combined with a-C film. The results demonstrated that the lubrication performance of polyether oils coupled with a-C film surpassed that of paraffin oil across various lubrication regimes. Notably, the stable superlubricity (friction coefficient<0.01) was achieved under mixed lubrication regime on the a-C film when coupled with polyethylene glycol (PEG) oil after a prolonged sliding duration. The tribo-induced formation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) at the steel/a-C friction interface lubricated by PEG oils was firstly observed. The hydroxylation of the counter ball surface and the fabrication of CQDs might jointly contribute to the achievement of the robust superlubricity of the PEG oil/a-C film hybrid lubrication system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amorphous carbon film
Tribology
Hybrid lubrication
Hydroxylation
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Minimum oil film thickness and lubrication regime
Microstructure and properties of amorphous carbon film
SEM and TEM characterizations of worn surface
Characterizations of wear debris in base oils

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the financial support of National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51865017), Double Thousand Plan of Jiangxi Province (jxsq2023102148) and Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (No. 20202BABL204044). We also are greatly appreciated for the material source of a-C films from the research group of Junying Hao at State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image of morphology of surface (a) and fractured cross section(b), Raman spectrum and nanoindentation displacement-load curves of the amorphous carbon film.

(a) Friction curves of the a-C film under different lubrication conditions, (b) average friction coefficients the a-C and bare steel sheets under dry sliding and oil lubrication, and (c) the friction curves of the a-C film coupling with PEG oil [The rotatory speed is 800 rpm, the average friction coefficients in (b) were calculated from the data of three tribo-tests, and more friction curves of the repeated tests are showed in Fig. S1 in supporting information].
(a-c) Frictional curves at various lubrication regime with different sliding speeds, (d-f) average friction coefficient and oil film thickness which is calculated according to the HD equation.
(a-d) 3D morphologies (left column) and (e-h) the cross-section profilers (right column) of wear traces on the a-C films under different lubrication states (rotatory speed: 800 rpm).

SEM images (left two columns) and the C and O elemental mapping images (right two columns) of the wear scars on steel balls sliding (rotatory speed: 800 rpm) against a-C film without (a-d) and with paraffin (e-h), PPG(i-l) and PEG (m-p) oils lubrication.

SEM images of (a) FIB cutting region on the steel ball sliding (rotatory speed: 800 rpm) in PEG oil and (b) the ion thinned slice for TEM observation, (c) TEM image of the cross section of the frictional region, and (d) Pt, (e) Fe, (f) C and (g) O elemental mapping images acquired through EDX.

Raman spectra of the wear spots on steel ball rubbing against a-C film under (a) dry sliding, (b) paraffin oil lubrication, (c) PPG oil lubrication and (d) PEG oil lubrication (rotatory speed: 800 rpm).

(a-b) TEM images of wear debris collected from the PEG oil, (c)micro photoluminescence spectra acquired at fresh steel ball covered with fresh PEG oil as control sample and the wear debris region on the steel ball sliding against a-C film in PEG oil, (d) TEM images of wear debris collected from the PPG oil after tribo-test (rotatory speed: 800 rpm), and (e-g) EDS elemental mapping images of Fe, O and C of the wear debris in PPG oil [Inserted image in (a) is particle size statistical results of 100 CQDs, inserted one in (b) is the IFFT image of the CQD marked with the red square, and inserted ones in (c) were the optical images fresh and tribo-tested steel ball, respectively, PL spectra were acquired at the position marked with green and red circles].
Viscosity-pressure coefficient (α), kinematic viscosity (η) and molecular structure diagrams of the tested base oils.

head.ce:title: Solid/liquid hybrid lubrication behaviors of amorphous carbon film coupling with nonpolar and polar base oils

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, 212003, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The amorphous carbon film (a-C) has aroused intensive interest in solid-oil hybrid lubrication design due to its low friction, high wear resistance, and good chemical inertness. However, the solid/liquid hybrid lubrication behaviors and mechanisms of a-C films when coupled with nonpolar and polar oils of similar viscosity are still insufficiently understood and ambiguous to date. In this study, we compared the lubrication properties of nonpolar paraffin oil and polar polyether oils with different molecular structures when combined with a-C film. The results demonstrated that the lubrication performance of polyether oils coupled with a-C film surpassed that of paraffin oil across various lubrication regimes. Notably, the stable superlubricity (friction coefficient<0.01) was achieved under mixed lubrication regime on the a-C film when coupled with polyethylene glycol (PEG) oil after a prolonged sliding duration. The tribo-induced formation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) at the steel/a-C friction interface lubricated by PEG oils was firstly observed. The hydroxylation of the counter ball surface and the fabrication of CQDs might jointly contribute to the achievement of the robust superlubricity of the PEG oil/a-C film hybrid lubrication system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The heightened energy dissipation and economic losses caused by friction and wear have drawn significant attention due to the ongoing improvements in power efficiency and emission standards across transportation and manufacturing industries [1]. Lubrication stands out as the most effective strategy to mitigate friction and wear in mechanical devices, underscoring its critical role in global sustainable development. Presently, oil lubricants remain the most commonly used materials to curb friction and wear in the industrial sector, thanks to their high reliability, cost-effectiveness, and ease of maintenance. However, apart from the risk of leakage, oils often suffer from the lean oil condition and fail to provide efficient lubrication during the frequent start-stop operations and the cold starts of mechanical equipment, resulting in the high friction and wear loss due to the too thin oil film on tribopair surfaces [2–4]. Additionally, the load capacity of oil-based lubrication is constrained by the hydrodynamic oil film, resulting in inadequate lubrication under high contact Hertz stress [5]. To address these issues, introduction of nano additives such as carbon quantum dots [6,7], graphene nanosheets [8,9], boron nitride nanosheets [10,11], black phosphorus nanosheets [12–14] and magnesium silicon hydroxide [15] have been proven an effective method to improve the tribological performance of different oils. Apart from the rolling, mending and polishing effects of nano additives [16], oil lubrication performance can also be significantly enhanced by the composite (synergistic) lubrication effect of the tribofilm induced from the tribochemical reactions of the nano additives with oil molecules. However, the low solubility and dispersion stability of nano additives are the main bottleneck for long service life in different oils. Especially, the formation of tribofilm derived from the nano additives usually needs a relatively long running period and causes wear loss of the tribopairs to obtain the solid-oil lubrication effect.

Alternatively, the solid-oil hybrid lubrication effect can be facilely acquired through deposition of solid coatings or films on tribopair surface. Compared to effects of nano additives on the lubrication performance of oils, the solid-oil hybrid lubrication based on the deposited solid coatings such as metal disulfide [5], metal boride [17], MXene [18] and diamond like amorphous carbon [19,20] coatings on friction pair surface has significant advantages in shortening the running-in period, reducing wear, and enhancing lubrication stability. Due to the protection and load capacity of the deposited anti-wear coatings, the server wear loss and high friction of the tribopairs can be avoided well even under lean oil conditions and high contact stress.

As classical solid lubricant films, the amorphous carbon films consisting of sp2 and sp3 hybridization have attracted intensive interests in the field of tribological research due to high chemical inertness, low friction and superior anti-wear resistance. The nanostructure, mechanical and tribological properties of a-C films can be tailored by doping of metal (e.g. Ti, W) and nonmetal (e.g. N, Si) [21–23] elements and regulating the deposition conditions. In particular, the solid lubrication of a-C films can be promoted to the super-low friction or superlubricity level (friction coefficient <0.01) by the elemental co-doping and nanostructure design, as reported in our previous works [24,25]. Nevertheless, the a-C films still confront the high environmental sensitivity of tribological performance within the long service duration of mechanical systems. Fortunately, this shortcoming of amorphous carbon films can be mitigated by combining them with oil lubricants, as the oil film can isolate the carbon film from the air. Different types of amorphous carbon film were found to exhibit various solid/liquid hybrid lubrication performance. For instance, Yan et.al [26] found that the DLC films exhibited a better hybrid lubrication than graphite like carbon (GLC) film when combining with ionic liquid lubricants. Moreover, it has been reported that the doping of different elements and compounds has also great influence on the hybrid lubrication performance of DLC films [27,28]. Takuya et.al compared the lubrication behaviors of organic friction modifiers under boundary lubrication, and the results showed that the ultra-low friction was realized by using unsaturated fatty acids on tetrahedral amorphous carbon films, while the friction coefficients of saturated fatty acids are much higher [29]. However, the emphasis is usually given to the effects of microstructure of a-C films and additives of oils on the hybrid lubrication performance, and the comparative studies of hybrid lubrication performance of the nonpolar and polar base oils with similar viscosity on the a-C films still are unclear until present.

In this work, the hybrid lubrication performance of paraffin (nonpolar) and polyether (polar) oils combined with an a-C film was investigated under different lubrication regimes. The results showed that the hybrid lubrication performance of polyether oils was superior to that of nonpolar paraffin oil across various lubrication regimes on the a-C films. Meanwhile, the tribological performance of the polyethylene glycol (PEG) oil/a-C film composite system was significantly better than that of the polypropylene glycol (PPG) oil/a-C film system, despite both oils being polar and having similar viscosities. The PEG oil/a-C film hybrid lubrication system demonstrated stable superlubricity after a prolonged sliding duration. It was found for the first time that the CQDs were in-situ fabricated at the steel/a-C friction interface lubricated by PEG oils. The effects of molecular structure on the solid/liquid hybrid lubrication performance of polyether oils and lubrication mechanisms were elucidated based on the comparative characterizations of the counter surface sliding in different oils.

The a-C films without hydrogen were fabricated by magnetron sputtering graphite targets in argon atmosphere on mirror polished stainless steel (ASTM304, 1 mm in thickness, Roughness: 20.1 nm) and Si(n100) substrates. The substrates were ultrasonically cleaned for 15 min in alcohol (Purity: analytical reagent) and acetone (Purity: analytical reagent), respectively. To enhance the adhesion of the a-C films, the chromium interlayer with about 200 nm thickness was deposited on the substrate before the deposition of a-C films. The sputtering currents of the graphite and chromium targets were controlled to 3.5 A and 4 A, respectively. Meanwhile, the substrates were charged with the negative bias voltage of 60 V. More detailed deposition processes can be in Ref. [26]. The paraffin oil (Purity: 99 %), PEG (Number-average molecular weight: 200) and PPG oils (Number-average molecular weight: 200) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. The viscosity-pressure coefficient (α), kinematic viscosity (η) and molecular structure diagrams of the tested oils are listed in Table 1 . Paraffin oil and polyether oils were selected in this work in the consideration of that these oils are representative of nonpolar and polar base oils in lubricant industry and have similar viscosity. So, the influence of viscosity of the oils on the hybrid lubrication performance can be minimized.

Friction tests were done on a customed tribometer (WTM-2E, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences) under ball-on-disk mode to evaluate the lubrication behaviors of different oils on the bare steel sheets (ASTM304, 1 mm in thickness, Roughness: 20.1 nm) and a-C film coated sheets. The GCr15 steel ball (Diameter: 6 mm, Roughness: 14 nm) is used as the counter ball. The normal load of the trbio-tests is set to 2 N, corresponding to the maximum Hertz contact stress of 850 MPa for the steel/steel tribopair, and 795 MPa for the steel/a-C tribopair. The rotatory speed was controlled to the range of 50–1000 rpm and the diameter of the rotatory trace is 8 mm, resulting in the linear speed of 0.021–0.42 m/s. Note that the tribopairs were not replaced with new ones during the continuous regulation of the rotatory speed. Base oil drops with 10–20 μL were covered on the steel sheets with and without a-C films. The relative humidity of the test environment was controlled to 50 ± 5 %. Three friction tests were repeated for each sample at the same conditions.

Surface morphology and fractured cross section of the a-C film were surveyed by atomic force microscope (AFM, Dimension Icon, Bruker, USA) and scanning electron microscope (SEM, MLA650F, FEI, USA). Raman spectra (HR800) of the deposited a-C film and wear scars on the steel balls sliding in different oils were detected at the excitation wavelength of 532 nm with power of 1 mW. Hardness and elastic modulus of the a-C film were acquired on a nanoindentor with a diamond tip (U9820A G200, Agilent, Apex-radius: 20 nm). In order to restrain the effect of substrate, the maximum indent depth was controlled to 10 % of the film thickness. The wear spots on the steel balls were characterized by SEM (MLA650F, FEI, USA) equipped with a EDX spectroscopy (Bruke, USA). The microstructure of the cross section of the wear scar on the steel ball were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA), and the thinned slice for TEM observation was obtained by focused ion beam (FIB) cutting and Ga ion thinning. The platinum layer was deposited at the cutting region before the ion beam cutting. The steel balls were immerged in solvent to clean the residual oils before transferred into FIB chamber. The wear debris for TEM observations were collected from residual polyether oils diluted with anhydrous ethanol at contact region on the steel balls. The wear volume of the a-C film lubricated by different oils was analyzed by a 3D profiler (UP-Lambda, Rtec Co., Ltd., USA). Wear rates of the a-C films under different lubrication conditions were calculated through the wear volume divided by normal load and sliding distance. The lase excited micro-photoluminescence (PL) spectra (Horiba LabRam HR Evolution) of the wear debris around contact spots on steel ball were acquired at the excitation wavelength of 325 nm with the laser power of 10 mW.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Minimum oil film thickness and lubrication regime
ce:section.ce:para:
The minimum oil film thickness (h min) was calculated according to Eqs. (1) and (2) [30],
(1)
h min
=
3.63
α 0.49
R 0.466
η 0.68
u 0.68
1 −
1
ⅇ
0.68 k
E ′
0.117
F 0.073
(2)
E ′
=
2
1 −
υ 1 2
E 1
+
1 −
υ 2 2
E 2
where k is a coefficient approximately equal to 1, R is the radius of steel ball, E 1 (185 GPa) and E 2 (219 GPa) are the elastic modulus of the a-C film and the steel ball, u is the linear sliding speed of the tribopairs (0.021–0.42 m/s), F is the normal load (2 N), v
1 and v
2 are the Poisson's ratios of the a-C film and steel ball, respectively. The pressure-viscosity coefficients (α), and kinematic viscosity (η), of the base oils are listed in Table 1. The lubrication regimes of the triboparis lubricated by base oils at the different sliding speeds were determined by Eq. (3),
(3)
λ =
h min
σ 1
2
+
σ 2
2
where σ 1 and σ 2 are the average surface roughness of the steel ball and a-C film, respectively. According to the value range of λ, the lubrication regimes of the oil lubricated tribopairs can be classified as elastohydrodynamic lubrication (λ > 3), mixed lubrication (1 ≤ λ ≤ 3), and boundary lubrication (λ < 1) [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure and properties of amorphous carbon film
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1(a) shows SEM and AFM (inset) images of the film surface morphology. It can be seen that the amorphous carbon film has a rough surface(average roughness: 7 ± 0.3 nm). The deposited amorphous carbon film possesses a column like structure along the direction of fractured cross section, as shown in Fig. 1(b). The growth of the column structure could result in the relatively rough surface of amorphous carbon film. The fitted results of Raman spectrum show that the intensity ratio of D and G peaks, I(D)/IG is as high as 2.5. It means that the sp2 carbon is the dominated phase in the amorphous carbon films [32]. The hardness(H) and elastic modulus(E) of deposited film are not very high due to the sp2 carbon dominated structure, as seen in Fig. 1(d), in contrast to diamond like carbon and tetrahedral amorphous carbon films with high sp3 carbon content [33].

Fig. 2(a) compares the frictional curves of the amorphous carbon films in different lubrication conditions at the rotatory speed of 800 rpm. Under dry sliding, the a-C film exhibits a high friction coefficient of 0.19. In sharp contrast, the friction coefficients of the steel/a-C tribopair significantly decrease when the base oils are introduced. Moreover, it can be found that the steel/a-C tribopairs lubricated by different oils demonstrate various friction performance. As seen in Fig. 2(b), the average friction coefficients using paraffin, PPG and PEG oils are about 0.086, 0.033 and 0.009. Based on the comparative outcomes of the steel/a-C tribopair under both dry friction and oil lubrication conditions, it can be concluded that there is a synergistic lubrication effect between the a-C film and base oils. More significantly, the steel/a-C tribopair lubricated with PEG oil achieved a state of superlubricity with a friction coefficient lower than 0.01 after a sliding distance of 1500–2500 m, as illustrated in Fig. 2(c). In contrast, the steel/a-C tribopair lubricated with paraffin and PPG oils did not experience such a state of superlubricity over the prolonged sliding duration.

The effects of sliding speed on the lubrication performance of the tested oils were further comparatively studied. Fig. 3 (a-c) show the frictional curves at the different rotatory speeds, which lead to the various lubrication regimes as determined by the calculated λ according to the Hamrock-Dowson equation [30]. It is obvious that the friction coefficients of the steel/a-C tribopair in all the oils decrease with increasing sliding (rotational) speed. This reduction could be attributed to the increased oil film thickness, as indicated in Fig. 3(d-f), which lessen solid asperity contact at the sliding interface under oil lubrication. However, it is noteworthy that the friction coefficients of the steel/a-C tribopair in PEG oil are much lower than those in other oils across different sliding speeds, with superlubricity being achieved only in PEG oil under mixed lubrication conditions. These frictional results suggest that the synergistic lubrication performance of the polar oils coupling with the a-C film is better than that of nonpolar oils at different sliding speeds, despite they have similar viscosity. In particular, PEG oil demonstrated the most favorable synergistic lubrication effect with the a-C film, as evidenced by its notably lower friction coefficient and the attainment of superlubricity compared to the other oils under mixed lubrication conditions.

The anti-wear performance of the a-C film in different oils was further studied at a rotational speed of 800 rpm after 2.4 × 105 sliding cycles (corresponding to a distance of 6000 m). Figs. 4 (a-h) show the 3-dimensional morphologies and cross-section profiles of the wear traces on the amorphous carbon films under different friction conditions at the rotational speed of 800 rpm. The a-C film exhibited varying anti-wear performance. Specifically, the maximum depth of the wear trace and the wear rate of the film under dry sliding were 1150 nm and 4.2 × 10−6 mm3/(N·m), respectively. However, under oil lubrication, the wear traces were shallower and the wear rate was lower. Meanwhile, the depth and wear rate of the a-C film decreased significantly under PEG oil lubrication compared to paraffin and PPG oil lubrication. Notably, no obvious wear loss of the a-C film lubricated by PEG oil was detected, indicating that the a-C film exhibits superior wear resistance when combined with PEG oil. The effective separation of solid asperity contact by the liquid lubricant film at the sliding interface plays a crucial role in reducing wear loss of the tribopairs [34]. Compared to paraffin and PPG oils, the lower wear rates of the a-C film under PEG oil lubrication suggest that the steel/a-C tribopair is more effectively separated by the PEG oil film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM and TEM characterizations of worn surface
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphologies of the wear scar on the counter ball were observed by SEM. As evident in Fig. 5 (a-c), the wear scar on the counter ball is very rough, lacking the formation of continuous transfer layers, which are usually essential for amorphous carbon films to attain a low friction state under dry sliding [25,35].The results of oxygen mapping distribution in Fig. 5(d) indicate that the wear debris are highly oxidized, which may cause the formation of discontinuous amorphous carbon transfer layers and lead to high friction of the a-C film during dry sliding. In contrast, the wear spot on the counter ball sliding in oils appears smooth, as shown in Fig. 5(e-p). More importantly, it can be seen that C and O aggregate at the wear spot on the steel ball in PEG oil, as shown in Fig. 5(o-p), while a similar phenomenon is not observed on the steel balls sliding in paraffin and PPG oils. The aggregation of C and O implies that there could be some amorphous carbon phases containing oxides produced at the contact region on the steel ball sliding in PEG oil. However, this aggregation of C and O does not appear to lead to the formation of a solid layer rich in carbon and oxygen, as further revealed by the FIB-TEM characterizations of contact region at the direction of cross section. As depicted in Fig. 6 (a-g), no additional solid layer containing carbon and oxygen was detected between the deposited Pt protective layer and the steel substrate. Thus, it can be supposed that the aggregation of carbon and oxygen is not due to the formation of a new solid transfer layer on the steel ball but might stem from the adsorption of PEG oil molecules on the worn surface, even though the steel balls were intensively cleaned using a high-pressure washer before the SEM observations.

Raman spectrum is a powerful method to detect the chemical structure information of the tribopair containing a-C films [36]. Fig. 7 (a-d) demonstrate Raman spectra of the wear spots on the steel balls sliding against a-C film in different lubrication conditions. It can be seen that the peaks at 676 cm−1 and 1332 cm−1 of Raman spectrum of wear spot on the ball under dry sliding mainly stem from the Fe3O4 phases which is attributed to the oxidation of steel surface layer during the friction tests. The shoulder peak around 1500 cm−1 usually is assigned to the amorphous carbon phase, indicating that some amorphous carbon (a-C) phase was fabricated on the steel ball. The peak of the Fe3O4 phase can also be found on wear spots on the steel ball under paraffin, PPG and PEG oil lubrication. However, there is no obvious peak of amorphous carbon phase detected on the steel ball sliding in PPG oil, as seen in Fig. 7(c), further confirming the SEM results in Fig. 5(i-l). More Raman peaks are detected wear scars on the ball sliding in PEG oil. As demonstrated in Fig. 7(d), the peaks at 1500 cm−1 assigned to amorphous carbon phase and the CH peak are noticeable. Especially, note that both PPG and PEG oils contains hydroxyl group, but the peak around 3500 cm−1 assigned to hydroxyl group [37] was only detected at wear scars on steel ball sliding in the PEG oil. This implies that PEG oil is more beneficial to the hydroxylation of worn surface on the steel ball sliding on a-C film. In terms of the oil molecular structure, we note that there are many branched methyl groups in the polymer chains of PPG oil. So, it can be speculated that the polymer chain of PPG oil contains branched methyl groups which could result in some steric hindrance effect and reduce the absorption strength of polar groups with the steel substrate, leading to the low adsorption mass of the PPG oil at the friction interface. Such steric hindrance effect also happened to the ionic liquids with different molecular structure reported by Yan et. al [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of wear debris in base oils
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructure of wear debris at the liquid lubrication interface could also contribute to the solid-liquid synergistic lubrication performance. To investigate the wear debris, PEG and PPG oils were collected from the contact region of the sliding balls for TEM analysis. Interestingly, as seen in Fig. 8(a), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were observed in the PEG oil collected from the steel ball after a sliding distance of 6000 m (corresponding to a sliding duration of 300 min) on the a-C film. The lateral size of the quasi-spherical CQDs is mainly in the range of 3–5 nm. The HRTEM results, as seen in Fig. 8(b), showed that the interplanar spacing of the CQDs is about 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite. [39]. This means that the carbon dots have a graphite-like crystal structure. The tribo-induced CQDs were further evidenced by the lase excited photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the wear debris around the contact region on the steel balls. The excited PL spectrum of the wear debris around the contact region on the steel ball is at the range of 400–700 nm with a emission peak at the 500 nm, as shown in Fig. 8(c), and this is in good agreement with the typical PL spectra of the carbon quantum dots reported in previous works [39,40] . In marked contrast, the signal of PL spectrum acquired at the control sample, a fresh steel ball covered with PEG oil, is very weak.

However, it should be pointed out that most of the CQDs exhibited the amorphous carbon state, this can be further evidenced by the Raman spectrum in Fig. 7(d). Meanwhile, as reported in previous works [41,42], the CQDs have been facilely synthesized by the thermal decomposition and carbonization of PEG under prolonged heating. Mechanistically, the pyrolysis decomposition and carbonization of organics requires a high-energy input to initiate by heating, ultrasonication or microwaves and so on. Identically, the degradation and carbonization reactions of oil lubricants can also be triggered at the friction interface due to some tribo-catalytic effect, and this is so called tribo-chemical reactions [43,44]. Considering the negligible wear loss of the a-C film in PEG oil, we believe that the growth of CQDs mainly depends on the decomposition and carbonization of the PEG molecules. On the other hand, many large wear debris with irregular shape were observed in PPG oil, as shown in Fig. 8(d). The EDS elemental mapping images in Fig. 8(e-g) showed that the wear debris mainly consist of ferric oxide and amorphous carbon phases. The large wear debris at friction interface cause abrasion wear behaviors, resulting in the increase of the wear loss of the tribopairs [45].
Based on above analysis, the synergistic lubrication mechanisms are deduced as followings:

Firstly, it has been proved that the hydroxylation of the counter surface under oil lubrication can promote the friction reduction to a superlubricity level [46,47], and the polar oils containing hydroxyl group has been found to be more beneficial for the hydroxylation of counter in our previous works [48]. The hydroxylation of the friction interface should be the results of formation of hydrogen bonding network which mainly is determined to the activity of hydroxyl group of the liquid lubricants. The paraffin oil is a kind of nonpolar oil without hydroxyl group, and thus it is not able to induce the hydroxylation of the steel ball, as evidenced by the results of SEM and Raman spectrum. On the other hand, the PPG and PEG oils identically contains hydroxyl group, but the hydroxylation only happened to the steel ball sliding in PEG oil, as suggested by the results of Raman spectrum in Fig. 7(c-d). This could be due to that the steric hindrance effect of the branched methyl group hinders the adsorption of PPG molecules on the steel ball. Also, it can be believed that the adsorption of PEG oil on the a-C film is stronger than that of PPG and paraffin oils. As a result, the PEG oil exhibited the more intensive synergistic lubrication behaviors on the a-C film.

Secondly, the stability of the oil film is primarily determined by the adsorption of oil molecules on the friction interface, which significantly affects the synergistic lubrication performance of oil lubricants and the wear loss of tribopairs. A more stable oil layer at the rubbing interface results in lower friction and wear loss of the tribopairs. As mentioned above, paraffin oil consists of nonpolar molecules without any polar groups, leading to weak adsorption and low stability of the oil film on the friction pairs. This implies that the paraffin oil film is not stable enough to afford an efficient separation for the solid asperity contact of the steel/a-C friction interface. So, the wear loss of a-C film lubricated by paraffin oil was still relatively high, as seen in Fig. 4(b) and (f), even when the oil film at the steel/a-C interface is as thick as 57 nm. In addition, the steric hindrance of branched methyl group of PPG molecules restrains the adsorption of the PPG oil at steel/a-C film tribopair interface even though the PPG oil are polar containing hydroxyl groups. As a result, the wear loss of a-C film in PPG oil could also be attributed to the instability of the PPG oil film. On the other hand, the negligible wear loss of the a-C film lubricated by PEG oil reflects the high stability of the PEG oil film to afford the robust separation of solid asperity contact of the steel/a-C tribopairs.

Thirdly, the tribo-induced carbon-based layer on the counter ball usually is essential for solid-oil composite superlubricity system [49,50]. Unexpectedly, the carbon-based transfer layer on the steel ball is not fabricated on the steel ball sliding in PEG oil, as seen in Fig. 6(a-g). This means that the carbon-based transfer layer is not dispensable for the steel/a-C tribopair to achieve superlubricity in PEG oil. On the other hand, we found that the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were in situ fabricated by the tribo-induced decompositions of PEG oil at the sliding interface, as confirmed by TEM characterizations of PEG oil collected from the steel/a-C tribopair. As matter of fact, CQDs have been widely applied to enhance the lubrication performance oil lubricants as additives [41,51,52]. Specially, as reported by Du et. al, the running-in period to reach superlubricity of the steel/steel tribopair lubricated by PEG-critic acid solution can be shortened by the introduction of CQDs [53]. Moreover, most of CQDs derived from PEG precursor usually are highly hydroxylated regardless of the synthesis routes [42]. Based on the high hydroxylation of CQDs, it can be assumed that the primary role of CQDs should be to enhance the stability of the PEG oil film at the tribopairs interface through the formation of hydrogen bonding network between CQDs and PEG oil molecules, which is beneficial to achieving the robust superlubricity.

The tribological properties of the solid-oil hybrid lubrication system, consisting of amorphous carbon film with paraffin and polyether base oils of similar viscosity, were comparatively studied. The results showed that polyether oils exhibited better synergistic lubrication effects when coupled with a-C film than nonpolar paraffin oil. Among the polar polyether oils, PPG oil demonstrated disadvantages in lubrication performance and reducing wear loss of tribopairs compared to PEG oil, despite having a similar molecular structure and viscosity to PEG oil. The steric hindrance effect of the branched methyl group on the hydroxylation and adsorption of PPG oil at the tribopair interface might be responsible for the inferior tribological performance of the PPG oil/a-C film lubrication system. More importantly, we found that the rough a-C film, when coupled with PEG oil, can achieve robust superlubricity and have negligible wear loss under a mixed lubrication regime. The in situ tribo-induced formation of carbon quantum dots in PEG oil was observed for the first time. The achievement of superlubricity in the PEG oil/a-C film solid-oil hybrid lubrication system can be attributed to the hydroxylation of the counter face and the formation of CQDs at the friction interface.

Yanfei Lin: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiaoqiang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jun Yang: Funding acquisition. Xinghua Zhang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support of National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51865017), Double Thousand Plan of Jiangxi Province (jxsq2023102148) and Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (No. 20202BABL204044). We also are greatly appreciated for the material source of a-C films from the research group of Junying Hao at State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111646.
